meta.name: EAT-26 Eating Attitudes Test
meta.description: The EAT-26 is a widely used 26-item self-report measure for screening abnormal eating attitudes and behaviors. It assesses dieting behavior, food preoccupation, and oral control, serving as an effective tool for identifying eating disorder risk. A score of 20 or above suggests further professional evaluation.
meta.author: Garner et al. (1982)
meta.reference: Garner DM, Olmsted MP, Bohr Y, Garfinkel PE. The Eating Attitudes Test: psychometric features and clinical correlates. Psychol Med. 1982;12(4):871-878.
meta.instruction: Please choose the response that best applies to each of the following statements.

questions.text: Am terrified about being overweight
Avoid eating when I am hungry
Find myself preoccupied with food
Have gone on eating binges where I feel that I may not be able to stop
Cut my food into small pieces
Aware of the calorie content of foods that I eat
Particularly avoid food with a high carbohydrate content (bread, rice, potatoes, etc.)
Feel that others would prefer if I ate more
Vomit after I have eaten
Feel extremely guilty after eating
Am preoccupied with a desire to be thinner
Think about burning up calories when I exercise
Other people think that I am too thin
Am preoccupied with the thought of having fat on my body
Take longer than others to eat my meals
Avoid foods with sugar in them
Eat diet foods
Feel that food controls my life
Display self-control around food
Feel that others pressure me to eat
Give too much time and thought to food
Feel uncomfortable after eating sweets
Engage in dieting behavior
Like my stomach to be empty
Have the impulse to vomit after meals
Enjoy trying new rich foods

scoring.description: Scoring: Always=3, Usually=2, Often=1, Sometimes/Rarely/Never=0. Item 26 is reverse scored.

ranges.label: Normal Range
ranges.description: Your eating attitudes are within the normal range, with no significant concerns about disordered eating patterns. You appear to have a generally healthy relationship with food and your body, without excessive preoccupation with weight or dieting.
suggestions: Continue maintaining balanced and flexible eating habits that nourish both your body and your enjoyment of food
Focus on overall nutritional well-being and physical activity rather than strict calorie counting or weight numbers
Be aware that eating attitudes can change during stressful periods; stay mindful of your relationship with food

ranges.label: Mild Concern
ranges.description: You show some elevated concern about eating, weight, or body image, though your score does not yet reach the clinical threshold. These attitudes may reflect common societal pressures around appearance, but monitoring for escalation is advisable.
suggestions: Be mindful of overly restrictive dieting behaviors that could develop into unhealthy patterns over time
Cultivate a healthy and flexible relationship with food by focusing on nourishment rather than restriction
If eating concerns are affecting your quality of life, mood, or social activities, consider consulting a professional
Challenge unrealistic body image standards promoted by media and social platforms

ranges.label: Further Evaluation Recommended
ranges.description: Your score reaches the clinical concern threshold of 20 or above, indicating possible eating disorder risk. This does not confirm a diagnosis but strongly suggests that professional evaluation by an eating disorder specialist is warranted.
suggestions: Consulting an eating disorder specialist or mental health professional for a comprehensive evaluation is strongly recommended
A score of 20 or above on the EAT-26 indicates possible eating disorder risk that should be taken seriously
Watch for behaviors such as extreme dietary restriction, binge eating, purging, or compulsive exercise
Monitor your physical health indicators including weight stability, menstrual regularity, and energy levels
Share your concerns with a trusted person who can support you through the evaluation process

ranges.label: High Concern
ranges.description: Your score is very high, strongly suggesting eating disorder-related attitudes and behaviors significantly affecting your health. Eating disorders are serious medical conditions, but they are treatable with proper professional care and support.
suggestions: Please seek evaluation and treatment from an eating disorder specialist as soon as possible
Eating disorders are serious but highly treatable conditions; recovery is achievable with appropriate professional support
Do not face this alone; inform a trusted family member or friend who can provide support and accountability
Monitor your physical health closely and avoid extreme dietary restriction, purging, or other compensatory behaviors
If you are in medical distress due to eating behaviors, seek emergency medical care immediately
